summary.tldr: Thariq demos a Remotion video build in Claude Code, covering design-system HTML porting, interview-style planning, /loop for continuous data, skills, verification, and Opus 4.7 quirks.
summary.overview: Thariq joins Jason for the hackathon's first live session, running an interactive Figma-board brainstorm and a live build while taking Discord questions throughout. The build ports Anthropic's site into a single HTML design-system file, then feeds it into a fresh Remotion project to generate a branded video with terminal, cursor, and button components. Along the way Thariq covers verification strategy, skills vs. sub-agents, claude.md drift, handling continuous data flows, Opus 4.7's more literal instruction-following, and how Claude Design compares to Claude Code for design work. Jason flags a follow-up session with Michael Cohen on Managed Agents the next day.

topics.title: Figma brainstorm intake
topics.summary: Thariq opens a Figma board collecting audience topics: video generation, voice agents, /loop, managed agents, continuous data.

topics.title: Design system to HTML
topics.summary: Porting a site's aesthetic into a single HTML design-system file as a portable artifact for future projects.

topics.title: Verification approaches
topics.summary: Using linting, custom scripts, and tools like Playwright to give Claude programmatic verification beyond screenshots.

topics.title: Interview-style planning
topics.summary: Prompting Claude to interview the user using ask-user-a-question to tighten vague specs before building.

topics.title: Claude Design vs Claude Code
topics.summary: Claude Design wraps opinionated design workflows; Claude Code offers more code-base context for custom projects.

topics.title: Continuous data flows
topics.summary: Using /loop with a since-filter script every 20 minutes; managed agents suggested for rapidly-changing state.

topics.title: Keeping claude.md fresh
topics.summary: Model updates (Opus 4.7 takes instructions more literally) require revisiting claude.md, skills, and over-constraining rules.

topics.title: Repeated mistakes and distribution
topics.summary: Claude repeats mistakes when pushed out of distribution; porting code or relaxing arbitrary style rules often helps.

qa.question: Since you can build so much, how do you decide not to build certain things?
qa.answer: Talk to users directly, watch them use it over their shoulder, and hold conviction while trying to disprove your own hypothesis rather than testing many at once.

qa.question: What custom skills have you built, and do you ship them on the marketplace?
qa.answer: Mostly unshipped personal workflow skills: reading Twitter DMs for bug signals, a Slack weekly status report, and a demo-video skill that reads new feature code and builds a Remotion demo.

qa.question: Can Claude review repeated work and auto-convert it into skills via a feedback loop?
qa.answer: Claude can read conversations in .claude/projects and propose skills, but output is often noisy; treat it as a human-in-the-loop suggestion rather than automation.

qa.question: How do you prevent Claude from writing more code than necessary?
qa.answer: Use plan mode and review carefully, try a /simplify skill that reads git changes to shrink code, keep claude.md tight, and be highly specific in prompts.

qa.question: How do tools, skills, memory, and the workflow engine relate?
qa.answer: Skills are the primary extension point; MCPs and agents supplement. Memory can be rolled via a skill — for example, a CRM skill that updates contacts, to-dos, and events from email and iMessage.

qa.question: How does Claude Design differ from Opus 4.7 with a well-defined prompt skill?
qa.answer: Claude Design is a wrapper with an opinionated workflow for ingesting design systems and strong defaults from designers like Nate, but Claude Code gives more code-base context.

qa.question: For an agent reevaluating state on new information, what pattern fits?
qa.answer: For clinic-style rapidly-changing interrelated data, a managed agent likely fits better than Claude Code, which is a coding harness assuming slower-moving context.

qa.question: Should agents in Claude Code get specific roles like front-end, back-end, ML?
qa.answer: Prefer skills over role-based agents — e.g., debugging skill, code quality skill. Over-constraining role prompts, especially with Opus 4.7, tends to backfire.

qa.question: How do you build a verification script that tests outputs against hard infrastructure constraints before sending?
qa.answer: Create a testing environment where Claude can iterate and write tests; only after passing verification should it send a real payload. Specifics depend on the task.

qa.question: How do you deal with Claude repeating the same mistakes?
qa.answer: Check if the ask is out of distribution or arbitrary style; check claude.md for conflicts; encode rules as linting where possible; use hooks to insert reminders dynamically.

qa.question: How do you keep claude.md conventions from going stale while building?
qa.answer: Revisit with each model — Opus 4.7 takes instructions more literally. Audit how often skills trigger, and use the Opus 4.7 prompt guide and migration skill on existing setups.

quotes.speaker: Thariq
quotes.text: I've been using HTML as a very portable way of essentially Claude compresses all of this information into an HTML file, and then you can use it in a different project.
quotes.why_it_matters: Frames the core trick behind the live build — a reusable design-system artifact.

quotes.speaker: Thariq
quotes.text: This is sort of my version of planning — I know there's some vagueness in my prompt, but I want Claude to help me think through it, so I ask it to interview me.
quotes.why_it_matters: Captures an alternative to plan mode using ask-user-a-question.

quotes.speaker: Thariq
quotes.text: It's easier to work what's in distribution with Claude than to work out of distribution.
quotes.why_it_matters: Summarizes his mental model for choosing languages, styles, and conventions.

quotes.speaker: Thariq
quotes.text: Opus 4.7 takes instructions more literally. If you find it over-triggering, usually it means you need to edit your claude.md to give it more flexibility.
quotes.why_it_matters: Concrete migration guidance for existing claude.md files.

quotes.speaker: Thariq
quotes.text: Skills over agents, and ask agents to combine skills. A lot of the 'you're an expert designer' prompting has gone away.
quotes.why_it_matters: Direct steer away from role-prompted sub-agents toward composable skills.

surprising.insight: Conversation history lives in .claude/projects as files Claude itself can read — enabling self-audits of how often skills trigger.
surprising.evidence: All your conversations exist in .claude/projects. Claude can read those files. You can prompt Claude to read it and give you tips.

surprising.insight: A personal CRM skill reads email and iMessage on a loop and updates contacts, to-dos, and events — a custom memory layer beyond Claude's default.
surprising.evidence: It stores contacts, to-dos, and events from reading my email plus iMessage… creating another memory layer for Claude Code.

surprising.insight: Dynamic hooks can be created by skills — e.g., a /safe skill that, once run, injects a reminder every turn or few turns.
surprising.evidence: Let's say you wanted /safe where every turn Claude gets reminded it's doing something dangerous. Once you run this skill, Claude gets this hook inserted.

surprising.insight: For Remotion specifically, programmatic verification can check whether video frames overlap — deterministic checks beyond Playwright screenshots.
surprising.evidence: In Remotion a lot of it is around video frames. How do I make sure different frames are not overlapping? If I can build a programmatic way of verifying that, Claude will get better.

surprising.insight: A /loop running every 20 minutes paired with a 'get-messages-since-last-fetch' script is the suggested pattern for continuous data ingestion in Claude Code.
surprising.evidence: Create a /loop. Every 20 minutes, check for new messages using get-messages-since, and do X. Maybe use the process-messages skill.

between_the_lines.reading: The repeated mention that 'not everyone wants to be interviewed' could hint at interview-style planning being considered for product but held back.
between_the_lines.evidence: We've been trying to figure out the right way to put this in product, but it's a little bit tricky because not everyone wants to be interviewed.

between_the_lines.reading: Framing claude.md maintenance as 'someone's job at large companies' suggests this drift problem is still unsolved tooling-wise.
between_the_lines.evidence: At large companies, this is someone's job, honestly, to make sure that the claude.md, or everything is up to date.

between_the_lines.reading: Pushback on role-based sub-agents as 'rigid' and 'over-constraining' could reflect internal learning that verbose agent prompts degrade Opus 4.7 specifically.
between_the_lines.evidence: If you have a lot of instructions for the agent, you sort of over-constrain Claude… especially to Opus 4.7.

between_the_lines.reading: The recurring 'we probably need to write an article about this' on verification suggests official guidance is still being drafted.
between_the_lines.evidence: We definitely need to make a verification post or article, I think.

feature_guides.name: Design-system-to-HTML porting
feature_guides.one_liner: Ask Claude to read a site or code base and compress its visual language into a single HTML design-system file reusable across projects.
details: Prompt used: 'look at the code and create a design system HTML file that fully represents the Anthropic design system.'
Output contained colors, typography, radiuses, shadows, buttons, toggles, chips, badges, and feedback states in one file.
The HTML file is then copied into a new project (e.g., a Remotion video repo) so the new build inherits the aesthetic.
Positioned as a hack for non-designers starting new projects.
feature_guides.speaker: Thariq
feature_guides.quote: I've been using HTML as a very portable way of essentially Claude compresses all of this information into an HTML file, and then you can use it in a different project.

feature_guides.name: Interview-style planning prompt
feature_guides.one_liner: Append 'interview me in depth about this using the ask-user-a-question tool' to vague prompts so Claude drives spec clarification.
details: Used in place of plan mode when the user hasn't fully thought through requirements.
Claude surfaces new ideas during interview (e.g., suggesting a mouse component Thariq hadn't considered).
Thariq mentions this could become a /interview skill, though productizing it is tricky since not every user wants to be interviewed.
feature_guides.speaker: Thariq
feature_guides.quote: Interview me in depth about this using the ask user a question tool. This is the type of prompt I like to do really often, is asking Claude to interview me.

feature_guides.name: /loop for continuous data
feature_guides.one_liner: Run a scheduled loop (e.g., every 20 minutes) that calls a since-timestamp fetch script and processes only new data.
details: Pair with a tight script like get-messages-since so the loop does nothing when there's no new data.
Example structure: every 20 minutes, check for new messages using get-messages-since, then invoke a process-messages skill.
Warning: can burn tokens if the script isn't tight.
For rapidly-changing, highly interrelated state (e.g., hospital data), a managed agent is suggested instead.
feature_guides.speaker: Thariq
feature_guides.quote: Create a slash loop, and then say every 20 minutes, check for new messages using get messages since, and do X.

feature_guides.name: /simplify skill
feature_guides.one_liner: A skill that reads existing git changes and tries to pare down unnecessary code.
details: Mentioned as Thariq's go-to when Claude writes more code than necessary.
Works against the current diff rather than the whole repo.
feature_guides.speaker: Thariq
feature_guides.quote: /simplify, which is a skill that Claude reads your existing Git changes and tries to simplify them. It works pretty well at shrinking down unnecessary code.

feature_guides.name: Programmatic verification scripts
feature_guides.one_liner: Give Claude domain-specific deterministic checks so it can verify its own work beyond generic linting or screenshots.
details: Generic linting is a starting point — push toward the most specific form of linting for the domain.
Remotion example: check that video frames don't overlap as a programmatic verification.
Playwright is useful but screenshots alone usually aren't enough information for Claude to act on.
For high-risk outputs (e.g., payments), build a testing environment where Claude iterates and writes tests before the real send.
feature_guides.speaker: Thariq
feature_guides.quote: If I can build a programmatic way of verifying that, then Claude will get better at doing that sort of verification. So write specific scripts to help with verifying things that Claude runs into often.

feature_guides.name: CRM skill (personal memory layer)
feature_guides.one_liner: An opinionated custom memory skill that ingests email and iMessage to maintain contacts, to-dos, and events.
details: Thariq mentions he's been working on open-sourcing it.
Runs on a loop: check for new messages/emails, then update contacts/to-dos/events.
Presented as an example of augmenting Claude's generic memory with opinionated structure via skills.
feature_guides.speaker: Thariq
feature_guides.quote: CRM. It stores contacts, to-dos, and events from reading my email plus iMessage. This is an example of creating another memory layer for Claude code.

feature_guides.name: Status report skill
feature_guides.one_liner: A skill that reads Slack and builds a weekly status report to send to a manager or post in a personal channel.
details: One of several business-workflow skills Thariq maintains.
Other examples: a Twitter DM/notification reader that correlates bug reports with Slack, and a demo-video skill that reads new feature code and builds a Remotion demo.
feature_guides.speaker: Thariq
feature_guides.quote: Status report. Read Slack and build a weekly status report on what I did this week. So I can just send that to my manager or just even post it on my personal Slack channel.

feature_guides.name: .claude/projects transcript access
feature_guides.one_liner: Claude can read its own conversation history stored in .claude/projects to audit workflows and propose skills.
details: Use it to ask Claude for tips on how you could work better — but prompt very specifically.
Can be asked to propose new skills, though output tends to be noisy with low-value suggestions.
Also usable to count how often each skill triggers when auditing a setup.
feature_guides.speaker: Thariq
feature_guides.quote: All your conversations exist in .claude/projects. Claude can read those files. You can prompt Claude to read it and give you tips, but you need to be very specific.

feature_guides.name: Dynamic hooks via skills
feature_guides.one_liner: A skill can install a hook that injects reminders every turn or every few turns for the remainder of a session.
details: Example given: a /safe skill that, once invoked, reminds Claude each turn it's doing something dangerous and must double-check.
Referenced in Thariq's skills article as a way to insert reminders dynamically rather than statically in claude.md.
feature_guides.speaker: Thariq
feature_guides.quote: Once you run this skill, then Claude will get this hook that's inserted, and you can insert it after every turn or after every few turns.

feature_guides.name: Opus 4.7 claude.md migration
feature_guides.one_liner: Opus 4.7 takes instructions more literally, so over-triggering usually means claude.md needs relaxed wording.
details: Caps-locked 'never do this' style rules can cause over-indexing on Opus 4.7.
Anthropic has published an Opus 4.7 prompt guide and a migration skill to point at existing skills.
Audit pattern: ask Claude to read .claude/projects and check how often each skill triggers.
feature_guides.speaker: Thariq
feature_guides.quote: Opus 4.7 takes instructions more literally. So sometimes when you ask it to do something, if you find Opus 4.7 is over-triggering on something, usually means you need to edit your Claude.md to give it a little bit more flexibility.

feature_guides.name: Skills over role-based sub-agents
feature_guides.one_liner: Prefer composable skills (debugging, code quality, feature design) over role-prompted sub-agents like 'front-end agent'.
details: Role prompts like 'you're an expert designer' are largely obsolete — Claude doesn't need to be told to write good code.
Verbose agent instructions over-constrain Claude, especially on Opus 4.7.
Preferred pattern: main Claude Code spawns sub-agents that assemble skills at the right moment for the task.
feature_guides.speaker: Thariq
feature_guides.quote: Skills over agents, and ask agents to combine skills... instead of a software engineering agent, you have debugging skill, code quality skill, maybe feature design/interview skill.

feature_guides.name: Claude Design vs Claude Code
feature_guides.one_liner: Claude Design is an opinionated wrapper around the model with strong design defaults; Claude Code has more code-base context.
details: Claude Design connects a GitHub repo and ingests a design system similarly to the HTML-porting pattern shown in the demo.
Defaults tend to look better out of the box thanks to Nate and the design team.
Claude Code is preferred when you want to point at a specific code base; Claude Design is harder to steer at arbitrary repos.
Video work in Claude Design was inspired by Thariq showing Nate how Anthropic's launch videos are built.
feature_guides.speaker: Thariq
feature_guides.quote: The benefit of Claude Design is that it has a lot of these small design things sprinkled in... So the designs tend to look better out of the box, but when you do it in Claude code, it has a little bit more context.

feature_guides.name: Remotion for launch-style videos
feature_guides.one_liner: Anthropic's launch videos are generated by Claude Code using the Remotion library — pairs well with a pre-built design-system HTML.
details: The live build set up a Remotion project with a custom player, terminal component, cursor, and button showcase.
Jason suggests builders use this approach for hackathon demo videos at the end of the week.
Combining design-system HTML + Remotion is what makes Claude-generated videos match Anthropic's polish.
feature_guides.speaker: Thariq
feature_guides.quote: All our launch videos are generated by Cloud Code. And we use this library called Remotion.

feature_guides.name: Managed Agents follow-up session
feature_guides.one_liner: Michael Cohen is running a dedicated Managed Agents session the morning after, West Coast time.
details: Bring Managed Agents questions there — including the clinic-style continuous-state use case Thariq deferred.
Session video will be posted in the Discord resources channel for other time zones.
feature_guides.speaker: Jason Bigman
feature_guides.quote: We're going to have a live session tomorrow specifically on Managed Agents as well.
